inputs.story: Drissel's Tortoise Farm is a refuge for tortoises from all over the world, many of which can live to be well over 100 years of age. Using only the clues below, match each tortoise on the farm to its species and age, and determine its country of origin. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Snappy is 18 years younger than the swoopbacked tortoise.
2. The 68 year old animal is the horned tortoise.
3. Chewie is 32 years old.
4. Of Snappy and Toredo, one is 14 years old and the other is the horned tortoise.
5. The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie.
inputs.constants:
ages: 14; 32; 50; 68.
tortoises: Chewie; Snappy; Speedy; Toredo.
species: black neck; horned; pitch belly; swoopbacked.
inputs.solution:
Ages	Tortoises	Species
14	Snappy	pitch belly
32	Chewie	swoop-backed
50	Speedy	black neck
68	Toredo	horned

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Drissel's Tortoise Farm is a refuge for tortoises from all over the world, many of which can live to be well over 100 years of age. Using only the clues below, match each tortoise on the farm to its species and age, and determine its country of origin. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Snappy is 18 years younger than the swoopbacked tortoise.
2. The 68 year old animal is the horned tortoise.
3. Chewie is 32 years old.
4. Of Snappy and Toredo, one is 14 years old and the other is the horned tortoise.
5. The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie.

Constants:
ages: 14; 32; 50; 68.
tortoises: Chewie; Snappy; Speedy; Toredo.
species: black neck; horned; pitch belly; swoopbacked.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program in Clingo format that solves a specific logic grid puzzle. The program must follow the provided examples and rules strictly.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Story**: Drissel's Tortoise Farm. Match tortoise, species, and age. Determine country of origin. Wait, the constants list doesn't include countries! Let me check the problem statement again.
   - "match each tortoise on the farm to its species and age, and determine its country of origin." But the constants listed are:
     - ages: 14; 32; 50; 68.
     - tortoises: Chewie; Snappy; Speedy; Toredo.
     - species: black neck; horned; pitch belly; swoopbacked.
     - There is NO country category in the constants list. This is a common artifact in these puzzles where the story mentions something not used in the actual grid, or it's a trick/mistake in the prompt. I will strictly follow the constants provided. I'll map tortoise, species, and age.
   - **Clues**:
     1. Snappy is 18 years younger than the swoopbacked tortoise. -> `age(Snappy) = age(swoopbacked) - 18`
     2. The 68 year old animal is the horned tortoise. -> `age=68 <-> species=horned` (or just `age(68), species(horned)` for same tortoise)
     3. Chewie is 32 years old. -> `age(Chewie) = 32`
     4. Of Snappy and Toredo, one is 14 years old and the other is the horned tortoise. -> Snappy and Toredo are different. One has age 14, the other has species horned.
     5. The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie. -> `age(pitch belly) < age(Chewie)`

   - **Constants**:
     - ages: 14, 32, 50, 68
     - tortoises: Chewie, Snappy, Speedy, Toredo
     - species: black neck, horned, pitch belly, swoopbacked

3.  **Determine Predicates & Structure**:
   - Categories: `tortoise`, `age`, `species`.
   - Predicate: `match(T, A, S)` or `assign(T, A, S)` or `tortoise_info(T, A, S)`. Let's use `match(tortoise, age, species)`.
   - Search space: `{match(T, A, S): age(A), species(S)} = 1 :- tortoise(T).`
   - Uniqueness: `{T1=T2; A1=A2; S1=S2} = 0 :- match(T1,A1,S1), match(T2,A2,S2), (T1,A1,S1) != (T2,A2,S2).`
   - Clue encoding:
     1. Snappy is 18 years younger than the swoopbacked tortoise.
        `A1 = A2 - 18 :- match("Snappy", A1, S1), match(T2, A2, S2), S2="swoopbacked".`
        Wait, S1 can be anything, T2 can be anything. This works.
     2. The 68 year old animal is the horned tortoise.
        `S="horned" :- match(T, A, S), A=68.`
        `A=68 :- match(T, A, S), S="horned".`
        Or just one direction is enough if uniqueness is enforced, but it's safer to state both or use equivalence. Actually, `match(T, 68, "horned")` is implied. Let's use: `S="horned" :- match(T, A, S), A=68.` and `A=68 :- match(T, A, S), S="horned".`
     3. Chewie is 32 years old.
        `A=32 :- match("Chewie", A, S).`
     4. Of Snappy and Toredo, one is 14 years old and the other is the horned tortoise.
        - Snappy != Toredo (already enforced by uniqueness, but maybe explicit? Not needed if search space is correct, but rule 5 says "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" -> three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D. I'll follow the pattern.)
        - Actually, the rule says: `{S="horned"; A=14}=1 :- match("Snappy", A, S).`
        - `{S="horned"; A=14}=1 :- match("Toredo", A, S).`
        - And they must be different tortoises (already handled by uniqueness, but I can add `match("Snappy",_,_) :-` wait, uniqueness covers it).
        - Wait, the clue implies exactly one of them is 14 and the other is horned. So Snappy is (14 or horned), Toredo is (14 or horned), and they are different. Since 14 != horned (different categories), this works.
     5. The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie.
        `A1 < A2 :- match(T1, A1, "pitch belly"), match("Chewie", A2, S2).`

   Let's refine clue 4: "Of Snappy and Toredo, one is 14 years old and the other is the horned tortoise."
   This means:
   - Snappy has age 14 or species horned.
   - Toredo has age 14 or species horned.
   - Snappy != Toredo (guaranteed by grid)
   - Also, the one with age 14 is NOT the one with species horned (obviously, different categories).
   So:
   `{A=14; S="horned"} = 1 :- match("Snappy", A, S).`
   `{A=14; S="horned"} = 1 :- match("Toredo", A, S).`
   This matches the pattern in example 2 rule 3: `{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".`

   Let's check clue 1 again: `Snappy is 18 years younger than the swoopbacked tortoise.`
   `A_snappy = A_swoop - 18`
   `A1 = A2 - 18 :- match("Snappy", A1, S1), match(T2, A2, S2), S2="swoopbacked".`
   This works.

   Clue 2: `The 68 year old animal is the horned tortoise.`
   `S="horned" :- match(T, A, S), A=68.`
   `A=68 :- match(T, A, S), S="horned".`

   Clue 3: `Chewie is 32 years old.`
   `A=32 :- match("Chewie", A, S).`

   Clue 5: `The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie.`
   `A1 < A2 :- match(T1, A1, "pitch belly"), match("Chewie", A2, S2).`

   Wait, is there a country category? The problem says "determine its country of origin" but constants don't list any. I will ignore country as per the provided constants list. Sometimes puzzles have extra flavor text. I'll stick to the given constants.

   Let's verify ages: 14, 32, 50, 68.
   Snappy = swoopbacked - 18.
   Possible ages for swoopbacked: 32, 50, 68. (14-18 negative)
   If swoopbacked=32, Snappy=14.
   If swoopbacked=50, Snappy=32.
   If swoopbacked=68, Snappy=50.
   Chewie=32.
   If Snappy=32, conflict with Chewie=32. So swoopbacked != 50.
   So swoopbacked is 32 or 68.
   If swoopbacked=32, Snappy=14.
   If swoopbacked=68, Snappy=50.
   Clue 2: 68 is horned.
   Clue 4: Snappy and Toredo are 14 or horned.
   If Snappy=14, then Toredo must be horned (so age 68).
   If Snappy=50, then Snappy is neither 14 nor horned (horned is 68). This would violate clue 4 for Snappy. So Snappy must be 14.
   Therefore: Snappy=14, swoopbacked=32.
   Toredo=horned=68.
   Chewie=32. But swoopbacked=32. So Chewie is swoopbacked.
   Remaining tortoise: Speedy. Remaining age: 50. Remaining species: pitch belly.
   Check clue 5: pitch belly (Speedy, 50) < Chewie (32)? 50 < 32 is false.
   Wait, contradiction? Let's re-read carefully.
   "The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie." -> age(pitch belly) < age(Chewie).
   If Chewie=32, pitch belly must be 14. But Snappy=14. Conflict.
   Did I misinterpret clue 4? "Of Snappy and Toredo, one is 14 years old and the other is the horned tortoise."
   Maybe Snappy is horned (68) and Toredo is 14?
   If Snappy=68 (horned), then swoopbacked = Snappy + 18 = 86. Not in ages.
   So my deduction holds: Snappy=14, swoopbacked=32. Chewie=32. So Chewie is swoopbacked.
   Then pitch belly < Chewie(32) -> pitch belly is 14. But Snappy is 14. Conflict.
   Wait, maybe "Chewie is 32 years old" is clue 3.
   Let's check ages again: 14, 32, 50, 68.
   Maybe I misread clue 1: "Snappy is 18 years younger than the swoopbacked tortoise." -> Snappy = swoopbacked - 18.
   Maybe clue 5: "The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie." -> age(pitch belly) < 32. So pitch belly = 14.
   But Snappy = 14. So Snappy = pitch belly.
   Then Toredo = horned = 68.
   Remaining: Speedy = 50, species = black neck.
   Let's check consistency:
   Snappy: 14, pitch belly.
   Chewie: 32, swoopbacked.
   Toredo: 68, horned.
   Speedy: 50, black neck.
   Clue 1: Snappy(14) is 18 younger than swoopbacked(32). Correct.
   Clue 2: 68 is horned. Correct.
   Clue 3: Chewie is 32. Correct.
   Clue 4: Snappy(14) and Toredo(68/horned). One is 14, other is horned. Correct.
   Clue 5: pitch belly(14) < Chewie(32). Correct.
   Everything works! The solution is consistent.

   Now, encode in ASP.

   Constants:
   `tortoise("Chewie"; "Snappy"; "Speedy"; "Toredo").`
   `age(14; 32; 50; 68).`
   `species("black neck"; "horned"; "pitch belly"; "swoopbacked").`

   Predicate: `match(T, A, S)`
   Search space: `{match(T, A, S): age(A), species(S)} = 1 :- tortoise(T).`
   Uniqueness: `{T1=T2; A1=A2; S1=S2} = 0 :- match(T1,A1,S1), match(T2,A2,S2), (T1,A1,S1) != (T2,A2,S2).`

   Clues:
   1. `A1 = A2 - 18 :- match("Snappy", A1, S1), match(T2, A2, S2), S2="swoopbacked".`
   2. `S="horned" :- match(T, A, S), A=68.`
      `A=68 :- match(T, A, S), S="horned".`
   3. `A=32 :- match("Chewie", A, S).`
   4. `{A=14; S="horned"} = 1 :- match("Snappy", A, S).`
      `{A=14; S="horned"} = 1 :- match("Toredo", A, S).`
   5. `A1 < A2 :- match(T1, A1, "pitch belly"), match("Chewie", A2, S2).`

   `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `{<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `:- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`

   Let's double check clue 4 pattern. The prompt says:
   "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.

   Or just one direction is fine, but two is safer.

   Check clue 1: `A1 = A2 - 18 :- match("Snappy", A1, S1), match(T2, A2, S2), S2="swoopbacked".`
   This is correct.

   Let's assemble the ASP program carefully.

   ```asp
   % Constants
   tortoise("Chewie"; "Snappy"; "Speedy"; "Toredo").
   age(14; 32; 50; 68).
   species("black neck"; "horned"; "pitch belly"; "swoopbacked").

   % 5. The pitch belly tortoise is younger than Chewie.
   A1 < A2 :- match(T1, A1, "pitch belly"), match("Chewie", A2, S2).

   #show match/3.
   ```

   I'll add `T1 != T2 :- match("Snappy", _, _), match("Toredo", _, _).` just to be safe, though uniqueness handles it. Actually, the prompt says "three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D." I will follow it exactly.

   My uniqueness rule: `{T1=T2; A1=A2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(T1,A1,S1), match(T2,A2,S2), (T1,A1,S1)!=(T2,A2,S2).` Matches.

   Check clue 1 format: `A1 = A2 - 18 :- ...` This is a constraint rule (head is atom, body is condition). It's valid ASP.
   Check clue 5 format: `A1 < A2 :- ...` Valid.

   One minor point: In Clingo, `A1 = A2 - 18` might be interpreted as equality constraint. It's fine. Alternatively, I could use `#show` or just leave it. Clingo handles arithmetic in heads/bodies.

   Let's test mentally:
   `match("Snappy", 14, "pitch belly")`
   `match("Chewie", 32, "swoopbacked")`
   `match("Speedy", 50, "black neck")`

   Clue 1: 14 = 32 - 18. True.

   Clue 4: Snappy: 14 or horned? 14. True. Toredo: 68 or horned? horned. True.
   Clue 5: pitch belly(14) < Chewie(32). True.
   Uniqueness: all distinct. True.
   Solution unique.

   One detail: The prompt says "determine its country of origin" but no country constants are given. I will ignore it as instructed by the constants list. Sometimes these puzzles have a mismatch between story and constants. I'll stick to the provided constants.

   Check format of constants: "ages: 14; 32; 50; 68." -> `age(14; 32; 50; 68).`
   "tortoises: Chewie; Snappy; Speedy; Toredo." -> `tortoise("Chewie"; "Snappy"; "Speedy"; "Toredo").`
   "species: black neck; horned; pitch belly; swoopbacked." -> `species("black neck"; "horned"; "pitch belly"; "swoopbacked").`

   All good.

   Let's review the rules carefully:
   "Prefer integers: strip special characters..." -> ages are integers.
   "Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible..." -> tortoises and species are strings.
   "Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces: {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1)." -> Matches.
   "Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once: {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...)." -> Matches.
   "Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms..." -> Matches.
   "Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate." -> Matches.

   One thing: In Clingo, `A1 = A2 - 18` in the head might be parsed as a constraint `:- not (A1 = A2 - 18)`. It's standard.
   I will write it as:
   `A1 = A2 - 18 :- match("Snappy", A1, S1), match(T2, A2, S2), S2="swoopbacked".`

   For clue 2, I'll use:
   `S="horned" :- match(T, A, S), A=68.`

   ```asp
age(14; 32; 50; 68).
species("black neck"; "horned"; "pitch belly"; "swoopbacked").

% 1. Snappy is 18 years younger than the swoopbacked tortoise.

A=68 :- match(T, A, S), S="horned".
   ```
